14. ❓ Viva Questions

  1. What is the hierarchy of controls in occupational health?
  • Eliminate → Substitute → Engineering → Administrative → PPE (Most to Least Effective)
  1. What are the radiographic features of Silicosis?
  • Small Rounded Opacities (Simple), Progressive Massive Fibrosis (PMF), Eggshell Calcification of Hilar Nodes.
  1. How do you differentiate Occupational Asthma from Work-Exacerbated Asthma?
  • OA = New Asthma Caused by Work (Latent Period, Sensitiser/Irritant); WEA = Pre-existing Asthma Worsened by Work.
  1. What is the classic radiographic finding in Asbestosis?
  • Pleural Plaques (Calcified, Diaphragm), Basal Interstitial Fibrosis, Honeycombing (Late).
  1. What is the diagnostic criteria for Hand-Arm Vibration Syndrome (HAVS)?
  • Vibration White Finger (Raynaud's) + Sensorineural Symptoms (Numbness/Tingling), Stockholm Workshop Scale, Exposure History to Vibrating Tools.
  1. What is the characteristic audiometric finding in Noise-Induced Hearing Loss?
  • Bilateral Symmetric Sensorineural Hearing Loss with Notch at 4-6 kHz.
  1. What is the relationship between Asbestos and Mesothelioma?
  • Asbestos = Only Known Cause of Mesothelioma (Signature Cancer), All Types Carcinogenic, Latency 20-40 Years.
  1. What is the management of Hand-Arm Vibration Syndrome?
  • Stop Exposure, Warmth, Smoking Cessation, Nifedipine (If Severe), Avoid Cold/Vibration.
  1. What are the common causes of Occupational Contact Dermatitis?
  • Allergic: Nickel, Chromate (Cement), Epoxy, Rubber Accelerators, Formaldehyde; Irritant: Wet Work, Solvents, Cutting Oils, Acids/Alkalis.
  1. What are the criteria for diagnosing Occupational Asthma?
  • Work-Related Symptoms + Objective Evidence (PEF Variability >20%, Specific IgE, Specific Inhalation Challenge).
  1. What are the health surveillance requirements for Lead workers?
  • Blood Lead Level 3-6 Monthly, Action Level >20 µg/dL, Suspension >50 µg/dL, ZPP Monitoring.
  1. What is the difference between Occupational Asthma and RADS?
  • OA = Latent Period, Sensitiser; RADS = Single High Exposure → Immediate Onset, No Latency, Persistent Asthma.
  1. What are the prescribed diseases for Workers' Compensation (UK)?
  • Mesothelioma, Asbestosis, Silicosis, CWP, Byssinosis, Occupational Asthma, NIHL, HAVS, WRULDs, Contact Dermatitis, Occupational Cancers, Infectious Diseases (Lab/Animal Workers).

15. 🧠 Confusions & Mnemonics

Occupational Asthma vs RADSOA = Latent Period + Sensitiser; RADS = Single High Exposure → Immediate Onset, Persistent

topics.title: Soil Food Contamination
topics.html:
[!info] Davidson Ch 9 Alignment: Environmental Medicine → Soil & Food Contamination
FCPS/MRCP Focus: Heavy metal soil contamination (Pb, Cd, Hg, As), POPs (dioxins, PCBs, OCPs), radioactive contamination, urban gardening risks, foodborne pathogens, chemical contaminants (mycotoxins, heavy metals, pesticide residues, veterinary drugs), food additives, HACCP, Codex Alimentarius, foodborne outbreak investigation

1. 🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Identify Soil Contaminants: Heavy metals (Pb, Cd, Hg, As), POPs (dioxins, PCBs, OCPs), radioactive contamination
  • [ ] Assess Urban Gardening Risks: Soil testing, raised beds, phytoremediation
  • [ ] Identify Foodborne Pathogens: Salmonella, Campylobacter, Listeria, E. coli, Clostridium, Norovirus
  • [ ] Identify Chemical Food Contaminants: Mycotoxins, heavy metals, pesticide residues, veterinary drugs, food additives
  • [ ] Apply Food Safety Systems: HACCP, Codex Alimentarius, foodborne outbreak investigation
  • [ ] Apply Regulatory Frameworks: National/EU/CODEX standards, MRLs, ADIs

2. 📖 Soil Contamination & Health

Major Soil Contaminants

ContaminantSourceHealth EffectKey Metric
Lead (Pb)Leaded petrol (historical), paint, batteries, smelting, industryNeurodevelopmental (children IQ loss), CVD, renalSoil: <400 ppm (residential), Blood Pb <5 µg/dL
Cadmium (Cd)Phosphate fertilisers, batteries, plating, waste incinerationRenal (tubular), Osteomalacia (Itai-Itai), Cancer (lung)Soil: <3 ppm, Urinary Cd <1 µg/g creat
Mercury (Hg)Chlor-alkali, gold mining, coal combustion, waste incinerationNeurotoxicity (MeHg), renal, developmentalSoil: <1 ppm, Hair Hg <1 ppm
Arsenic (As)Pesticides (historical), mining, smelting, geogenicSkin lesions, cancer (skin, lung, bladder), CVD, diabetesSoil: <20 ppm, Water <10 µg/L
POPs: Dioxins/FuransWaste incineration, industrial processes, chlorine bleachingCancer (TCDD Group 1), Chloracne, Endocrine, ImmuneTEQ (WHO-TEQ) pg/g
POPs: PCBsTransformers, capacitors, hydraulic fluids, old building materialsCancer, neurodevelopmental, endocrine, immuneCongener-specific TEQ
OCPs (DDT, Dieldrin, Aldrin, etc.)Historical agricultural useEndocrine disruption, cancer, neurotoxicity, eggshell thinningVarious MRLs
RadionuclidesNuclear accidents (Chernobyl, Fukushima), weapons testing, miningCancer (thyroid, leukaemia), ARS, geneticBq/kg, Dose (mSv/yr)
[!tip] Soil Contamination = Ingestion (hand-to-mouth), Inhalation (dust), Dermal, Food Chain Transfer. Children = High Risk (pica, hand-to-mouth, higher absorption).

3. 📖 Urban Gardening & Food Production Risks

RiskSourceMitigation
Lead in SoilHistorical leaded petrol, paint, industryTest soil, Raised beds with clean soil, Mulch, Wash produce
Cadmium/ArsenicPhosphate fertilisers, former orchards, smeltingSoil testing, pH management (liming reduces Cd uptake), Avoid high-Cd crops (rice, leafy)
PAHs / DioxinsUrban deposition, incineratorsPeel root vegetables, Wash thoroughly, Avoid root crops in contaminated soil
MicrobiologicalManure, sewage sludge, irrigation waterCompost properly (time/temperature), Wash produce, Gap between application/harvest
[!warning] Urban Gardening on Contaminated Soil = Significant Exposure Route. Test Soil Before Planting. Root Vegetables > Leafy > Fruits for contaminant uptake.

4. 📖 Foodborne Pathogens — Major Agents

PathogenSource / VehicleIncubationKey Clinical Features
Salmonella (non-typhoidal)Poultry, eggs, meat, produce6-72hDiarrhoea, fever, cramps
Campylobacter jejuniPoultry, raw milk, water2-5 daysBloody diarrhoea, fever, GBS trigger
Salmonella Typhi/ParatyphiWater, food, carriers1-3 weeksTyphoid fever (step-ladder fever, rose spots)
E. coli O157:H7 (EHEC)Beef, produce, petting zoos3-4 daysBloody diarrhoea, HUS (children)
Listeria monocytogenesDeli meats, soft cheese, smoked fish, produce1-70 daysMeningitis, sepsis, pregnancy loss, Cold growth
Clostridium botulinumCanned foods, honey, vacuum-packed12-36hDescending flaccid paralysis, Dysphagia, diplopia
Clostridium perfringensMeat, gravy, reheated foods8-16hWatery diarrhoea, cramps, self-limiting
Bacillus cereusRice (emetic), meats/vegetables (diarrhoeal)1-6h (emetic), 8-16h (diarrhoeal)Two syndromes: Emetic (vomiting), Diarrhoeal
Staphylococcus aureusCream pies, meats, salads (enterotoxin)1-6hRapid vomiting, self-limiting
Vibrio parahaemolyticusRaw seafood (oysters)4-96hWatery diarrhoea, abdominal cramps
NorovirusShellfish, ready-to-eat, person-to-person12-48hVomiting, diarrhoea, outbreaks (cruise ships, institutions)
Hepatitis AShellfish, produce, water, person-to-person15-50 daysAcute hepatitis, jaundice

5. 📖 Chemical Food Contaminants

Mycotoxins (Fungal Metabolites)

MycotoxinProducing FungiMain FoodsHealth Effect
Aflatoxins (B1, B2, G1, G2)Aspergillus flavus, parasiticusGroundnuts, maize, tree nuts, spices, dried fruitHepatocellular Carcinoma (Group 1), Acute hepatotoxicity
Ochratoxin AAspergillus ochraceus, Penicillium verrucosumCereals, coffee, wine, dried fruit, spicesNephrotoxic, Carcinogenic (Group 2B)
Fumonisins (B1, B2)Fusarium verticillioides, proliferatumMaize, maize productsOesophageal cancer, Neural tube defects
Deoxynivalenol (DON / Vomitoxin)Fusarium graminearum, culmorumCereals (wheat, barley, maize)Gastrointestinal (vomiting), Immunosuppression
Zearalenone (ZEN)Fusarium graminearumCereals, maizeOestrogenic (reproductive toxicity)

Heavy Metals in Food

MetalMain Dietary SourcesHealth ConcernKey Regulation
LeadCereals, vegetables, water, spices, game meat (shot)Neurodevelopmental (children)ML: 0.01-0.3 mg/kg (varies by food)
CadmiumCereals, vegetables, shellfish, offal, smokersRenal (tubular), Osteomalacia (Itai-Itai)ML: 0.01-0.2 mg/kg
Mercury (Methylmercury)Fish (predatory: tuna, swordfish, shark)Neurodevelopmental (fetal/infant)ML: 0.5-1.0 mg/kg (fish)

Pesticide Residues & Veterinary Drugs

CategoryConcernsRegulation

Pyrethroids / NeonicotinoidsLower acute toxicity, bee toxicity, environmental persistenceMRLs, Pollinator Risk Assessment
GlyphosateControversial carcinogenicity (IARC 2A)MRLs, Re-evaluation ongoing

Food Additives & Adulterants

ColourantsTartrazine, Sunset Yellow, CarmineHyperactivity (controversial), Allergy

AdulterantsMelamine (milk), Sudan dyes (spices), industrial dyesFraud, Acute/Chronic Toxicity

6. 📖 Food Safety Systems — HACCP & Codex

HACCP — 7 Principles

PrincipleAction
1. Hazard AnalysisIdentify biological, chemical, physical hazards at each step
2. CCP IdentificationCritical Control Points where control is essential
3. Critical LimitsMeasurable criteria (time, temp, pH, Aw) at each CCP
4. MonitoringPlanned observations/measurements at CCPs
5. Corrective ActionsPre-defined actions when CCP limit exceeded
6. VerificationProcedures to confirm HACCP works (audits, testing, reviews)
7. DocumentationRecords of HACCP plan, monitoring, corrective actions

Codex Alimentarius — International Food Standards

ComponentRole
General StandardsLabelling, additives, contaminants, hygiene, Methods of Analysis
Commodity StandardsSpecific foods: Milk, meat, fish, cereals, fruit/veg, fats/oils
Pesticide Residues (MRLs)Codex MRLs → Basis for National/International Trade (WTO SPS)
Veterinary Drug Residues (MRLs)Codex MRLs → Withdrawal Periods, Acceptable Daily Intake (ADI)
Food Additives (INS/JECFA)ADI, Maximum Levels, Technological Justification
Contaminants (MLs)Lead, Cadmium, Arsenic, Mercury, Tin, Mycotoxins, PAHs, PAHs, Dioxins

7. 📖 Foodborne Outbreak Investigation

Steps

StepAction
1. DetectionSurveillance (Syndromic, Lab, Complaints), Cluster Detection
2. Case DefinitionClinical + Lab + Epidemiological (Person, Place, Time)
2. Hypothesis GenerationInterviews (Food History), Case-Control Study
4. Hypothesis TestingAnalytical Study (Case-Control / Cohort), Statistical Association
5. Source IdentificationTraceback / Traceforward, Environmental Sampling, WGS Comparison
6. Control MeasuresRecall, Recall, Public Alert, Facility Correction, Exclusion
7. ReportingLocal/National/International (INFOSAN, RASFF, PulseNet)

8. 🌍 Climate Change & Food Safety

ImpactExamples
Temperature RiseIncreased Microbial Growth (Salmonella, Vibrio), Mycotoxin Production (Aflatoxin in maize)
Extreme WeatherFloods → Contamination, Drought → Concentration of Contaminants
Ocean WarmingVibrio spp. Expansion (Cholera, Vibrio wound infections), HABs (Ciguatera, PSP)
CO2 RiseReduced Nutrient Density (Protein, Zn, Fe in crops)
Pest/Disease ShiftsNew Pests → Increased Pesticide Use, New Mycotoxin Risks

10. ❓ Viva Questions

  1. What are the main heavy metals contaminating soil and their health effects?
  • Pb (Neurodevelopmental), Cd (Renal/Osteomalacia), Hg (Neurotoxicity), As (Cancer, Skin lesions, CVD)
  1. What is the main risk of urban gardening on contaminated soil?
  • Direct ingestion of soil/dust, Inhalation of dust, Consumption of contaminated produce (root vegetables highest uptake).
  1. Which mycotoxin is most strongly associated with hepatocellular carcinoma?
  • Aflatoxin B1 (Group 1 Carcinogen) — Synergistic with Hepatitis B.
  1. What are the key heavy metals regulated in food and their main sources?
  • Pb (Cereals, veg, water, spices), Cd (Cereals, veg, shellfish, offal), Hg (Predatory fish), As (Rice, water, seaweed).
  1. What are the main mycotoxins and their health effects?
  • Aflatoxins (HCC), Ochratoxin A (Nephrotoxic), Fumonisins (Oesophageal Ca, NTD), DON (GI), ZEN (Endocrine).
  1. What are Maximum Residue Limits (MRLs) and who sets them internationally?
  • Codex Alimentarius Commission (FAO/WHO) sets Codex MRLs; basis for WTO SPS Agreement.
  1. What is HACCP and its 7 principles?
  • Hazard Analysis → CCP Identification → Critical Limits → Monitoring → Corrective Actions → Verification → Documentation.
  1. How do you investigate a foodborne outbreak?
  • Detect → Define Cases → Hypothesis (Interviews) → Test (Case-Control) → Traceback/Traceforward → Control → Report.
  1. What are the climate change impacts on food safety?
  • ↑ Mycotoxins (Aflatoxin), ↑ Vibrio/cholera, ↑ Aflatoxin in maize, ↓ Crop nutrients, ↑ Pests/Pesticides.
  1. What is the difference between MRL and ADI?
  • MRL = Max legal residue in food; ADI = Acceptable Daily Intake (safe lifetime exposure).
  1. What are the Codex Alimentarius standards used for?
  • International Food Standards, MRLs, MLs, Additive ADIs → WTO SPS Agreement Reference.
  1. What is the most common cause of foodborne botulism and its clinical presentation?
  • Clostridium botulinum in improperly canned/preserved foods → Descending flaccid paralysis, dysphagia, diplopia, respiratory failure.

11. 🧠 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Aflatoxin vs Other MycotoxinsAflatoxin = Liver Cancer (Potent Carcinogen); Others: Kidney, Oesophagus, GI, Endocrine
Lead in Soil vs FoodSoil → Direct Ingestion/Dust + Plant Uptake; Food = Multiple Sources (Cereals, Water, Spices, Game)
Acute vs Chronic Heavy Metal ToxicityAcute = GI, Shock; Chronic = Neuro, Renal, Cancer, Endocrine
MRL vs ADIMRL = Legal Limit in Food; ADI = Safe Daily Intake for Lifetime
HACCP vs GMPHACCP = Hazard-Specific, Science-Based; GMP = General Hygiene/Manufacturing Practice
Codex vs National StandardsCodex = International Reference (WTO SPS); National can be Stricter
MnemonicMeaning
"Aflatoxin = Liver Cancer (HCC)"Aflatoxin Carcinogenicity
"Lead = Neurodev, Cadmium = Kidney/Bone, Mercury = Neuro (Fetal), Arsenic = Cancer/Skin"Heavy Metals
"HACCP = 7 Steps: HA → CCP → CL → Monitor → CA → Verify → Doc"HACCP Principles
"MRL = Legal Limit in Food; ADI = Safe Daily Dose"MRL vs ADI
"Codex = WTO SPS Reference"Codex Role
"Outbreak = Detect → Define → Hypothesis → Test → Trace → Control → Report"Outbreak Steps

topics.html:
[!info] Davidson Ch 9 Alignment: Environmental Medicine → Water Pollution & Waterborne Diseases
FCPS/MRCP Focus: Water quality parameters, microbiological/chemical contaminants, waterborne diseases (cholera, typhoid, hepatitis A/E, giardia, cryptosporidium), water treatment, water safety plans, WHO guidelines

1. 🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Identify Water Quality Parameters: Microbiological, chemical, radiological, physical
  • [ ] Classify Water Contaminants: Microbiological (bacteria, viruses, protozoa, helminths), chemical (heavy metals, nitrates, fluoride, pesticides, PFAS), radiological
  • [ ] Recognise Waterborne Diseases: Cholera, typhoid, hepatitis A/E, giardiasis, cryptosporidiosis, shigellosis
  • [ ] Apply Water Treatment: Disinfection (chlorination, ozonation, UV), filtration, coagulation, point-of-use
  • [ ] Apply Water Safety Plans (WSPs): WHO framework, hazard analysis, critical control points
  • [ ] Apply WHO Guidelines: Drinking-water quality (2017), recreational water, wastewater reuse

2. 📖 Water Quality Parameters & Contaminants

Categories of Contaminants

CategoryParametersHealth Significance
MicrobiologicalE. coli / Thermotolerant Coliforms (Faecal Indicator), Enterococci, Clostridium perfringens, Coliphages, Pathogens (Bacteria, Viruses, Protozoa, Helminths)Faecal Contamination, Pathogen Presence
ChemicalHeavy Metals (As, Pb, Hg, Cd, Cr), Nitrate/Nitrite, Fluoride, Pesticides, PFAS, Pharmaceuticals, DBPs (THMs, HAAs)Acute/Chonic Toxicity, Cancer, Endocrine Disruption
RadiologicalRadon, Uranium, Radium, Gross Alpha/BetaCancer Risk
Physical/AestheticTurbidity, Colour, Taste/Odour, pH, TDS, HardnessAcceptability, Treatment Efficiency Indicator
[!tip] E. coli / Thermotolerant Coliforms = Primary Faecal Indicator. Enterococci = Better for Marine/Recreational Water. Turbidity = Key for Filtration Efficiency & Disinfection Efficacy.

3. 📖 Water Contaminants — Health Significance

Chemical Contaminants of Major Concern

ContaminantSourceHealth EffectWHO Guideline Value
Arsenic (As)Geogenic (Groundwater), Mining, PesticidesSkin Lesions, Cancer (Skin, Lung, Bladder), CVD, Diabetes, Neurodevelopment10 µg/L
Lead (Pb)Plumbing (Pipes, Solder, Brass), Industrial, PaintNeurodevelopmental (Children IQ Loss), CVD, Renal10 µg/L
Mercury (Hg)Industrial, Mining, Coal CombustionNeurotoxicity (Methylmercury), Renal, Developmental6 µg/L (Inorganic)
Fluoride (F⁻)Geogenic (Groundwater), FluoridationDental/Skeletal Fluorosis (High), Caries Prevention (Optimal ~0.5-1.0 mg/L)1.5 mg/L
Nitrate / NitriteAgriculture (Fertilisers, Manure), Septic Tanks, WastewaterMethaemoglobinaemia (Infants), Potential Cancer50 mg/L (NO3-), 3 mg/L (NO2-)
FluorideGeogenic, FluoridationDental/Skeletal Fluorosis1.5 mg/L
Pesticides (Organophosphates, Organochlorines, Carbamates, Pyrethroids)Agriculture, Vector ControlAcute Toxicity, Neurotoxicity, Endocrine Disruption, CancerCompound-Specific
DBPs (THMs, HAAs)Chlorination of Organic MatterCancer (Bladder), Reproductive EffectsTHMs: 100 µg/L (Sum)
PFAS (PFOS, PFOA)Industrial, Firefighting Foam, Consumer ProductsImmune, Thyroid, Cancer, DevelopmentalProvisional: 100 ng/L (Sum PFOS+PFOA)
Pharmaceuticals / Endocrine DisruptorsWastewater, AgricultureEmerging Concern (Endocrine, Resistance)Under Review
[!warning] Arsenic = Major Global Problem (Bangladesh, India, Pakistan, Argentina, Chile, China, USA). Nitrate = Infant Methaemoglobinaemia (Blue Baby Syndrome). Fluoride = Dental/Skeletal Fluorosis (Endemic Areas).

4. 📖 Microbiological Waterborne Diseases

Major Waterborne Pathogens

PathogenDiseaseTransmissionKey Features
Vibrio cholerae O1/O139CholeraWater/FoodRice-Water Stools, Rapid Dehydration, Epidemic Potential
Salmonella Typhi / ParatyphiTyphoid / Paratyphoid FeverWater/FoodFever, Relative Bradycardia, Rose Spots, Hepatosplenomegaly
Shigella spp.Shigellosis (Dysentery)Water/Food/Person-to-PersonBloody Diarrhoea, Tenesmus, Fever, Toxic Megacolon
Salmonella non-typhoidalSalmonellosisFood/WaterDiarrhoea, Fever, Abdominal Cramps
E. coli (EHEC O157:H7)Haemorrhagic Colitis, HUSFood/Water/Animal ContactBloody Diarrhoea, HUS (Children)
Campylobacter jejuniCampylobacteriosisPoultry, Water, MilkBloody Diarrhoea, GBS Trigger
Hepatitis A VirusHepatitis AWater/Food/Person-to-PersonAcute Hepatitis, Jaundice, Self-Limited
Hepatitis E VirusHepatitis EWater (Genotype 1/2), Zoonotic (3,4)Acute Hepatitis, High Mortality in Pregnancy
Giardia lambliaGiardiasisWater (Cysts), Food, Person-to-PersonDiarrhoea, Steatorrhoea, Malabsorption
Cryptosporidium spp.CryptosporidiosisWater (Oocysts), Person-to-PersonWatery Diarrhoea, Immunocompromised: Severe/Chronic
Cyclospora cayetanensisCyclosporiasisFood/Water (Berries, Herbs)Prolonged Watery Diarrhoea
Entamoeba histolyticaAmoebiasisWater/Food (Cysts)Dysentery, Liver Abscess

5. 📖 Key Waterborne Disease Profiles

Cholera (Vibrio cholerae O1/O139)

AspectDetails
TransmissionWater/Food contaminated with faeces
Incubation2 Hours – 5 Days
ClinicalPainless Rice-Water Diarrhoea, Vomiting, Rapid Dehydration, Rice-Water Stools, No Fever
ComplicationsSevere Dehydration, Hypovolaemic Shock, Acute Renal Failure, Hypokalaemia
DiagnosisStool Culture (TCBS), Rapid Dipstick (VC-O1/O139), PCR
TreatmentORT (WHO-ORS) → IV Fluids (Ringer's Lactate) if Severe, Doxycycline 300mg Single Dose / Azithromycin 1g Single Dose / Ciprofloxacin
ControlWASH, Oral Cholera Vaccine (OCV) - Killed Whole Cell (Shanchol/Euvichol), Sanitation

Typhoid Fever (Salmonella Typhi)

FeatureDetails
TransmissionWater/Food contaminated by chronic carriers
Incubation1-3 Weeks
ClinicalStep-Ladder Fever, Relative Bradycardia, Rose Spots, Hepatosplenomegaly, Leukopenia
ComplicationsIntestinal Perforation, Haemorrhage, Encephalopathy, Relapse
DiagnosisBlood Culture (Gold Standard), Widal Test (Low Sens/Spec), PCR
TreatmentCeftriaxone 2g IV BD / Azithromycin 1g OD × 7d (Resistance Rising)

Hepatitis A & E

FeatureHepatitis AHepatitis E
TransmissionFaecal-Oral (Water, Food, Person-to-Person)Water (Genotype 1/2), Zoonotic (Genotype 3/4 - Pork/Game)
Incubation15-50 Days15-60 Days
ClinicalAcute Hepatitis, Jaundice, Self-LimitedAcute Hepatitis, High Mortality in Pregnancy (20-25%), Chronic in Immunocompromised
DiagnosisIgM Anti-HAVIgM Anti-HEV, RNA PCR
PreventionHep A Vaccine (2 Doses), Hygiene, Safe WaterHygiene, Safe Water, Cook Pork Thoroughly (Genotype 3/4)

Protozoal Parasites

ParasiteKey FeaturesDiagnosisTreatment
Giardia lambliaDiarrhoea, Steatorrhoea, Bloating, MalabsorptionStool Antigen ELISA (Gold Standard), PCRMetronidazole 400mg TDS × 5-7d / Tinidazole 2g Single Dose
CryptosporidiumWatery Diarrhoea, Immunocompromised: Severe/Chronic, Chlorine ResistantStool Antigen ELISA, PCR, Modified Acid-Fast StainNitazoxanide 500mg BD × 3d, Supportive (Immunocompetent)
CyclosporaProlonged Watery Diarrhoea, Fatigue, AnorexiaModified Acid-Fast Stain (Autofluorescence), PCRTMP-SMX (Bactrim) DS BD × 7-10d

6. 📖 Water Treatment — Multi-Barrier Approach

Conventional Treatment Train

StepProcessPurposeKey Parameters
1. ScreeningScreens / MicrostrainersRemove Large Debris—
2. Coagulation / FlocculationCoagulants: Alum, Ferric Chloride, PAClDestabilise Colloids, Form FlocspH, Dose, Rapid Mix
3. Sedimentation / ClarificationSettling Tanks / Lamella Clarifiers / DAFRemove FlocsDetention Time, Surface Loading Rate
4. FiltrationRapid Sand / Dual Media / Membrane (UF/MF/RO)Remove Particles, MicrobesTurbidity <0.1-0.3 NTU, LRV
5. DisinfectionChlorination (Primary), Ozone, UV, ChloraminesPathogen InactivationCT Value, Residual
6. pH Correction / FluoridationLime / Soda Ash / CO2, FluorideCorrosion Control, Dental HealthpH 7.5-8.5, F⁻ 0.7-1.0 mg/L

Disinfection Methods

MethodMechanismAdvantagesLimitations
Chlorination (Cl₂, NaOCl, Ca(OCl)₂)Oxidation, HOCl/OCl⁻Residual Protection, Low Cost, EstablishedDBPs (THMs, HAAs), Taste/Odour, Less Effective on Protozoa Cysts
Chloramination (NH₂Cl)Chlorine + AmmoniaLonger Lasting Residual, Less DBPsWeaker Disinfectant, Nitrification, Lead Solubility
Ozonation (O₃)Strong OxidationStrongest Disinfectant, No Residual, Removes Taste/Odour/ColourNo Residual, Bryate Formation, High Cost, Complex
UV Irradiation (254 nm)DNA DamageNo Chemicals/DBPs, Effective on Protozoa (Crypto/Giardia)No Residual, Turbidity Dependent, No Residual Protection
Chlorine Dioxide (ClO₂)OxidationEffective on Biofilm, Less THMsChlorite/Chlorate Byproducts, Unstable
[!tip] Multi-Barrier = Best Protection. Filtration + Chlorination = Standard. UV/Ozone = Protozoa (Crypto/Giardia). Chlorine = Residual + Cost-Effective.

7. 📖 Point-of-Use (POU) Treatment

MethodTargetUse Case
BoilingAll PathogensEmergency, Household
Chlorination (Tablets/Liquid)Bacteria, VirusesEmergency, Travel
Ceramic FiltersBacteria, ProtozoaHousehold (LMIC)
Biosand FiltersBacteria, Protozoa, TurbidityHousehold/Community (LMIC)
SODIS (Solar Disinfection)Bacteria, VirusesLow-Resource, Clear Bottles + Sunlight 6h
Membrane Filters (UF/MF)Bacteria, ProtozoaHousehold, Emergency
Coagulant/Disinfectant Sachets (e.g., PUR)Coagulation + DisinfectionEmergency, Household

8. 📖 Water Safety Plans (WSP) — WHO Framework

⬇ Clinical Pathway
**WSP Development**
↓
**Team Assembly** (Multi-disciplinary)
↓
**System Description** (Source → Consumer)
↓
**Hazard Identification & Risk Assessment**
↓
**Critical Control Points (CCPs)**
↓
**Operational Monitoring** (CCP Parameters)
↓
**Corrective Actions** (Deviation Response)
↓
**Verification** (Audit, Water Quality Monitoring)
↓
**Documentation & Communication**
↓
**Review & Improvement** (Periodic, Incident-Triggered)

Key Components

ComponentDescription
System AssessmentSource Water Quality, Treatment Processes, Distribution System, Consumer Points
Hazard IdentificationMicrobiological (Pathogens), Chemical (Priority Chemicals), Physical/Radiological
Risk AssessmentLikelihood × Severity → Risk Matrix
CCPs (Critical Control Points)Points Where Control is Essential (e.g., Disinfection CT, Filter Turbidity, pH)
Operational MonitoringReal-Time/Online at CCPs (Turbidity, Chlorine Residual, pH, Flow)
Corrective ActionsPre-Defined Actions if CCP Limits Exceeded
VerificationWater Quality Monitoring, Audits, Consumer Complaints
Management ProceduresDocumentation, Training, Emergency Response, Record Keeping
[!tip] WSP = Proactive, Risk-Based, Multi-Barrier. CCPs = Points Where Loss of Control = Unacceptable Risk.

11. ❓ Viva Questions

  1. What is the indicator organism for faecal contamination of water?
  • E. coli / Thermotolerant Coliforms (Primary); Enterococci (Marine/Recreational)
  1. What is the treatment of choice for Cholera?
  • ORT (WHO-ORS) First, IV Ringer's Lactate if Severe, Doxycycline 300mg Single Dose or Azithromycin 1g Single Dose.
  1. What is the treatment for Typhoid Fever?
  • Ceftriaxone 2g IV BD or Azithromycin 1g OD × 7 Days.
  1. How is Cryptosporidium resistant to chlorine?
  • Oocysts Have Thick Wall, Highly Chlorine-Resistant, UV/Ozone Effective.
  1. What is the standard water treatment train?
  • Screening → Coagulation/Flocculation → Sedimentation → Filtration → Disinfection (Chlorination) → pH Correction.
  1. What is the CT value in water disinfection?
  • CT = Concentration (mg/L) × Contact Time (min); Measure of Disinfection Efficacy.
  1. What is the WHO guideline value for Arsenic in drinking water?
  • 10 µg/L.
  1. What is the point-of-use treatment for emergency water purification?
  • Boiling (1 min), Chlorine Tablets, SODIS (Clear Bottle + Sun 6h), Ceramic Filter, Coagulant/Disinfectant Sachets.
  1. What is a Water Safety Plan (WSP) and its key components?
  • Risk-Based Management: System Assessment → Hazard ID → CCPs → Operational Monitoring → Corrective Actions → Verification → Management Procedures.
  1. How do Cryptosporidium oocysts resist chlorination?
  • Thick Wall, Low Metabolic Rate, CT Value Required > Standard Chlorination Provides.
  1. What is the treatment for Typhoid Fever?
  • Ceftriaxone 2g IV BD or Azithromycin 1g OD × 7 Days.
  1. What is the WHO guideline for E. coli in drinking water?
  • 0 CFU/100mL (Must Not Be Detected in 100mL Sample).
  1. What is the arsenic guideline value and its main health effect?
  • 10 µg/L, Skin Lesions, Cancer (Skin/Lung/Bladder), CVD, Diabetes, Neurodevelopmental.

12. 🧠 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Cholera vs TyphoidCholera: Rice-Water Stools, No Fever, Rapid Dehydration; Typhoid: Step-Ladder Fever, Rose Spots, Relative Bradycardia
Hepatitis A vs EHep A: Self-Limited, Vaccine Available; Hep E: High Mortality in Pregnancy, Zoonotic (Genotype 3/4)
Giardia vs CryptoGiardia: Steatorrhoea, Cysts in Stool, Metronidazole/Tinidazole; Crypto: Chlorine Resistant, Immunocompromised Severe, Nitazoxanide
Cholera vs Typhoid VaccinesCholera: OCV (Killed WC, 2 Doses); Typhoid: Vi PS (Single) or Ty21a Oral (3 Doses)
POU MethodsBoiling = Gold Standard; Chlorine Tablets = Easy; SODIS = Sun + Clear Bottle; Ceramic = Household
MnemonicMeaning
"Cholera = Rice Water + ORS + Doxy"Cholera Management
"Typhoid = Ceftriaxone / Azithro"Typhoid Tx
"Crypto = Chlorine Resistant + UV/Ozone"Crypto Resistance
"Multi-Barrier = Coag → Sed → Filt → Disinfect"Treatment Train
"WSP = System → Hazard → CCP → Monitor → Verify"Water Safety Plan
"POU = Boil / Chlorine Tab / SODIS / Ceramic"Household Options
